pytorch_task_v1.prompt_instructions: Implement this ML task as a SINGLE self-contained Python file (task.py). Include all required functions AND a complete `if __name__ == '__main__':` block that trains the model, evaluates on a validation split, prints standard metrics (MSE, R2, parameter accuracy where applicable), and asserts quality thresholds so the script exits non-zero on failure. Do NOT create separate test files or README. The script itself is the test.
evaluation_rules: The `evaluate()` function MUST compute and return standard metrics: MSE, R2 score, and any task-specific metrics on the VALIDATION data split.

The `if __name__ == '__main__':` block MUST: (1) train the model, (2) evaluate on BOTH train and validation splits, (3) print all metrics clearly, (4) assert quality thresholds (e.g., R2 > 0.9, MSE < threshold), (5) exit 0 on success or non-zero on failure.
Do NOT use a separate test file. All evaluation logic goes in main().

tasks.description: Multifeature linear regression on a built-in real dataset with mini-batch training and learning-rate scheduling.

requirements.math: Document MSE objective and prediction equation y_hat = Xw + b in the task docstring.
requirements.data: Use built-in real data (prefer fetch_california_housing, fallback to load_diabetes if unavailable). Train/validation split is required.
requirements.implementation: Use torch.nn.Linear with mini-batch DataLoader and a learning-rate scheduler.
requirements.validation: On validation split, assert strong regression quality with dataset-appropriate R2 and RMSE thresholds.
requirements.output: Return a dict with train/val loss history and final validation metrics.

tasks.algorithm: Linear Regression (Robust Huber vs MSE, Real Data)
tasks.description: Train on outlier-corrupted targets from a built-in real regression dataset and compare MSE vs robust Huber regression.

requirements.math: Describe Huber loss and why it is less sensitive to outliers than MSE.
requirements.data: Use built-in real regression data (California Housing preferred; Diabetes fallback), then inject outliers into training targets only.
requirements.implementation: Train two PyTorch models with identical architecture: MSELoss baseline and SmoothL1Loss (Huber) robust model.
requirements.evaluation: Compute validation MAE, MSE, and R2 for both models.
requirements.validation: Assert robust model achieves lower validation MAE than MSE baseline under outlier corruption.

tasks.description: Binary classification on real built-in data (Breast Cancer) with BCEWithLogitsLoss and explicit L2 regularization.

requirements.math: Include sigmoid/log-loss and L2 penalty term in documentation.
requirements.data: Use sklearn.datasets.load_breast_cancer with train/validation split and feature standardization.
requirements.implementation: Train a baseline model (no L2) and a regularized model (explicit L2 penalty in loss).
requirements.metrics: Compute manual accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 on the validation split.
requirements.validation: Assert regularized model keeps high F1 while reducing weight norm relative to baseline.

tasks.algorithm: Logistic Regression (Polynomial Expansion on Real Data)
tasks.description: Use a built-in real dataset (Wine, binary setup) and compare linear logistic regression vs polynomial feature expansion.

requirements.math: Document polynomial feature map and logistic probability formula.
requirements.data: Use sklearn.datasets.load_wine, convert to binary class_0_vs_rest, and use two features for boundary visualization.
requirements.implementation: Train one logistic model on raw features and one on polynomial-expanded features.
requirements.visualization: Save linear vs polynomial decision boundary plot as an artifact.
requirements.validation: Assert polynomial-feature model outperforms linear baseline on validation accuracy/F1.
